segments.s281-g25: همی is a poetic/adverbial particle placed before the clause to mark iterative or continuous past ("kept/constantly"). The phrase زور و بخت is a coordinated noun phrase ("power and fortune") functioning as the object/theme that is perceived. از جهاندار is a prepositional phrase (از + noun) meaning "from/in/coming from the sovereign"; the past-tense verb دید (saw) closes the clause, so the whole clause reads literally “constantly saw power and fortune from the sovereign.”
segments.s281-g26: وز here is a literary contraction (و + از) meaning "and from," linking this clause to the previous one. آن گردش بخت is a noun phrase with گردش ("turn, revolution, circuit") governed by the genitive بخت ("Fate/fortune") and specified by آن ("that"), so “that turning of Fate.” بیدار is an adjectival predicate meaning "awake/alert," and دید (past "saw") is the verb; syntactically the speaker "saw (the turning of) Fate awake," i.e. perceived the awakening implied by Fate's turning.
segments.s281-g1: برون تاخت is a past-tense verbal phrase (تاخت, 3rd person past) meaning 'rushed/galloped forth'; the subject (هفتم) follows the verb in the typical epic/poetic inversion. ز + گردان هجیر is a genitive/origin phrase 'from the troop of Hojir' (گردان = troop, هجیر = proper name), indicating where that seventh came from.
segments.s281-g2: یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain / one' introducing an appositive; نامداری qualifies the following noun as 'renowned' and functions adjectivally/nominatively with سواری ('horseman, rider'). هجیر at the end is apposition: the name of that famed horseman.
segments.s281-g3: سپهرم is used as a personal name here (not the literal 'my sky'); ز خویشان افراسیاب is a genitive phrase 'of the relatives/kin of Afrasiab', marking lineage or affiliation.
segments.s281-g4: یکی نامور بود is a nominal predicate: یکی 'a certain' + نامور 'renowned' + بود (past of 'to be') = 'he was a famed one.' با جاه و آب is a prepositional phrase 'with rank and آب' (آب here figurative: standing/reputation), characterizing his status.
segments.s281-g5: ابا پور گودرز here functions as a personal-name group (Abā‑pūr, son of Goudarz) performing the action; رزم آزمود is a compound verbal phrase where رزم (battle) is the object and آزمود (past of 'to try/test') is the verb: 'tried battle' = 'engaged/tested himself in combat.'
segments.s281-g6: که چون introduces a causal subordinate clause ('for/since'); چون او به لشکر سواری نبود = 'since he was not in the cavalry/mounted force.' او is the subject 'he', به + لشکر سواری is the locative/organizational phrase 'in the army’s cavalry', and نبود is the past negative copula 'was not.'
segments.s281-g7: برفتند is 3rd-person plural past 'they went/advanced'; هر دو ('each two' = 'both') is the subject phrase following the verb, and به جای نبرد is the directional phrase 'to the place of battle' (به = to/toward, جای = place, نبرد = battle).
segments.s281-g8: برآمد (3rd sing. past) 'arose/issued forth' takes ز آوردگه ('from the place/venue') as its source; تیره گرد is a nominal phrase describing the force as 'dark/ominous' (تیره) + گرد (here part of the nominal phrase meaning a gathering/host in context), so the whole means 'an ominous/dark host rose up from the muster-place.'
segments.s281-g17: درافتاد is a simple past verb meaning 'fell' or 'fell down' (compound of در + افتاد); here it denotes the physical falling off the horse. ز introduces the source 'from' (poetic preposition). اسبش is 'his horse' (noun + enclitic possessive). هم آنگه is a temporal phrase 'then/at that moment' (هم giving emphasis and آنگه = then). نگون is an adjective meaning 'overthrown/downfallen' describing his state after the fall.
segments.s281-g18: به introduces the manner/circumstance ('in' or 'with'); زاری و خواری are a paired abstract nouns 'lamentation and humiliation/abasement' describing how he lay. دهن پر ز خون is a compact phrase 'mouth full of blood' where دهن 'mouth' is modified by پر (here 'full (of)') followed by ز + خون 'with/from blood' (poetic expression for 'filled with blood').
segments.s281-g19: فرود آمد is a past-tense verb phrase 'descended/dismounted' (فرود = descent, آمد = came). از governs the origin/source 'from'. باره here is an archaic/poetic noun meaning 'burden/load' or 'mount' (contextually referring to the rider's mount/horse or the place from which one alights). فرخ هجیر is the proper name (two words: فرخ as name-form and هجیر the personal name).
segments.s281-g20: مر is an archaic/discourse clitic here introducing the action (not freely translated in modern English). او را is the definite object 'him' (او + object marker را). ببست is simple past 3sg of بستن 'he bound/tied'. از بر زین = 'onto/from upon the saddle' (از بر = from/on) with زین 'saddle'. چو شیر is a simile 'like a lion'. The clause as a whole: '(thereupon) he bound him to the saddle like a lion.'
segments.s281-g21: نشست is the past tense 'sat down' or 'alighted/settled'. از بر = 'from off' (از + بر) and اسب = 'horse' — together 'alighted from the horse'. و links the next clause. آن اسب اوی = 'that horse of his' where آن = 'that', اسب = 'horse', and اوی is an archaic possessive 'his/her'.
segments.s281-g22: گرفته is the perfect/participle form of گرفتن 'having taken/seized' and modifies the following noun عنان 'reins' (i.e., 'having seized the reins'). و links the next participle درآورده ('having brought out/turned'), where درآورده (from درآوردن) here means 'turned/exposed', and روی 'face' completes it: 'having seized the reins and turned his face.' The construction uses two past participles to describe sequential actions.
segments.s281-g23: برآمد is past 'rose up' or 'ascended'. به بالا is an adverbial phrase 'upwards/on high' (به = to/toward, بالا = up/high). و links the next action. کرد آفرین is a fixed idiom 'he made/uttered praise' — i.e., he exclaimed 'bravo' or praised (آفرین as acclamation, کرد past of کردن).
segments.s281-g24: بر introduces the object of the praise 'upon/at' (کرد آفرین بر ... = 'he praised/said bravo to ...'). آن = 'that'. اختر = 'star' (here metaphorical/astrological). نیک و فرخ زمین = two adjectives 'auspicious (nīk) and fortunate (farrokh) of the earth/land' qualifying اختر; together they form 'that auspicious, fortunate star of the earth.'
segments.s281-g9: به شمشیر هر دو برآویختند places an instrumental prepositional phrase up front: به شمشیر = 'with/by sword' (به used instrumentally). هر دو = 'both (of them)' and برآویختند is 3rd person plural past (poetic) from برآویختن meaning 'to spring/attack upon'; the verb follows the subject phrase in this line of poetry.
segments.s281-g10: همی زآهن آتش فروریختند is a second coordinated clause: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle here ('indeed/just'), زآهن آتش is a genitive phrase 'fire/of iron' (i.e. sparks from the metal), and فروریختند is the 3rd person plural past of فروریزی/ریختن, 'fell/shattered/was showered down' agreeing with the (poetic) plural sense of the sparks.
segments.s281-g11: هجیر دلاور به کردار شیر is a nominal phrase: 'Hejir the valiant' (name + epithet), followed by a manner phrase به کردار شیر = 'in the conduct/manner of a lion.' Here به marks manner/instrumentality and کردار is 'behavior/conduct' functioning as the head of that phrase.
segments.s281-g12: به روی سپهرم درآمد دلیر is a verbal clause where به روی = 'upon/on the face of', سپهرم = 'my sky/heaven' (poetic), and درآمد is a past verb 'entered/appeared' (literally 'came in'); دلیر follows the verb as a predicative adjective 'brave.' Word order here places the locative phrase before the verb, then the verb, then its predicate.
segments.s281-g13: به نام جهان‌آفرین کردگار is a fixed invocation 'in the name of (the) world‑creator, the Maker.' به نام = 'in the name of' (به used idiomatically), and جهان‌آفرین کردگار is a titling apposition (two epithets in sequence) referring to God/the Creator.
segments.s281-g14: به بخت جهاندار با شهریار is a short prepositional cluster: به بخت = 'by/through the fortune' (به marking cause or instrument), جهاندار modifies بخت (the sovereign's fortune), and با شهریار = 'with the king' (با indicating accompaniment). Together they express agency/blessing tied to the ruler.
segments.s281-g15: یکی تیغ زد بر سر و ترگ اوی is a coordinated clause starting with an indefinite subject یکی = 'someone' or 'a certain one.' تیغ زد is 'struck with a sword' (تیغ as instrument/object, زد past 'struck'), بر سر = 'on the head.' و links to ترگ اوی ('his rag/shred') where اوی is the enclitic possessor 'his'; the second noun is elliptical (the verb/impact is implied on the rag).
segments.s281-g16: که آمد هم اندر زمان مرگ اوی is a relative clause introduced by که = 'which/that.' آمد is past 'came/happened,' هم adds emphasis 'also/even,' اندر زمان = 'in the time of,' and مرگ اوی = 'his death.' The clause modifies the preceding event, saying it occurred at the time of his death.

sentences.s281-s13: He constantly beheld power and fortune in the sovereign, and from that turning of Fate he saw fortune awaken.
sentences.s281-s1: Out rushed the seventh from Hojir’s troop — a certain famed horseman, Hojir.
sentences.s281-s2: Sepehram was of Afrasiab’s kin; he was a famed man, with rank and standing.
sentences.s281-s3: Abā‑pūr Goudarz tried his hand in battle, for he was not among the mounted troops.
sentences.s281-s4: Both went to the place of battle; from the muster-place there rose a dark/ominous host.
sentences.s281-s9: He then fell from his horse, cast down, and in lamentation and abasement his mouth full of blood.
sentences.s281-s10: Farrokh Hijir dismounted, and he bound him to the saddle like a lion.
sentences.s281-s11: He sat down from the horse, and that horse of his, having seized the reins, (he) turned his face.
sentences.s281-s12: He rose up on high and cried 'bravo' to that auspicious, fortunate star of the earth.
sentences.s281-s5: Both leapt at one another with their swords; sparks of fire fell from the iron.
sentences.s281-s6: Hejir the valiant, in the manner of a lion, appeared upon my sky — brave.
sentences.s281-s7: In the name of the world‑creator, the Maker; by the fortune of the sovereign, with the king.
sentences.s281-s8: Someone struck at his head with a sword, and his rag (turban) — which also occurred at the time of his death.
